text: The surgeon performs a robotic low anterior resection with a diagonal insertion of four trochars and an assistant trochar. The inferior mesenteric vein is identified and elevated, while the small bowel is medialized to the right side of the patient. The mesentery is entered in a medial to lateral approach beneath the inferior mesenteric vein, progressing towards the inferior mesenteric artery visible on the screen's right. The mesentery, containing fat and blood vessels, is dissected away from the retroperitoneum, revealing structures such as the left kidney, gonadal vessels, and ureter beneath.

The surgeon separates tissue planes to mobilize the mesentery, tenting up the mesentery of the transverse colon superior to the inferior mesenteric vein. A vessel sealing device is used to divide the inferior mesenteric vein, facilitating mesentery mobilization for pelvic connection. The arc of Rhyolan is identified and divided. Dissection continues to the splenic flexure, mobilizing lateral attachments of the colon from the splenic flexure and distal transverse colon, with omental fat held up. The spleen is visible in the corner of the view as omental attachments to the transverse colon are finished to ensure adequate reach into the pelvis.

The surgeon focuses on the pelvic region, identifying the right iliac artery with the right ureter and gonadal vessels crossing over it. The base of the mesentery of the sigmoid or recto-sigmoid colon is scored to enter the avascular plane, visible as a wispy layer. The superior rectal artery is held up, and the left iliac vessel, left ureter, and left gonadal vessels are visible. The inferior mesenteric artery, the main blood supply of the left colon and upper rectum, is isolated for high ligation near the aorta, ensuring lymph node involvement is addressed. The mesentery is separated from retroperitoneal structures, including the ureter and gonadal vessels.

The surgeon detaches the remaining lateral attachments of the abdominal colon, completing the mobilization. Attention is then directed towards the pelvis, where the black ink tattoo marks the tumor's location on the upper rectum. The surgeon enters the avascular plane posterior to the rectum along the sacrum, with the sacrum forming the floor of the view. The dissection continues down to the pelvic floor muscles for a complete resection, and the rectum is separated from the seminal vesicles and prostate.

The surgeon continues dissection around the rectum, working down towards the pelvic floor muscles visible at the bottom of the field. A robotic linear cutting stapler with two rows of staples and a blade is inserted to separate the rectum, sealing the ends with a 45mm stapler. The rectum is visibly sealed on both ends, preventing any spillage into the surgical field.

The surgeon pulls up the section of the colon containing the tumor from the pelvis, leaving the pelvis empty after rectum removal. The mesentery is separated up to the proximal colon above the tumor level, where reconnection to the rectum will occur. Endocyanin green dye is injected, revealing areas with good blood supply through laser imaging, while the isolated section does not light up. The pelvic floor is also illuminated. The procedure will be completed through a small incision.
